stemHe: מה פלט התוכנית הבאה: public class GenericStack<E>{ Stack<E> stk= new Stack<E>(); ... } class Output{ public static void main(String[] args) { GenericStack<String> gs= new GenericStack<String>(); gs.push("Rusia"); System.out.print(gs.pop()+" "); gs.push(2018); System.out.print(gs.pop()); } }
reason: This 2019 reconstruction transcribes the classic GenericStack joke question but, unlike every confirmed version of this question in 2018/2024/2025 (which always use TWO separate generic instances - one GenericStack<String> and one GenericStack<Integer>), this text shows only ONE instance 'gs' being reused, with gs.push(2018) called directly on the GenericStack<String> instance. Taken literally, this would actually be a genuine compile-time type-mismatch error (cannot push an int/Integer onto a Stack<String>) rather than the usual 'no error, prints both values' punchline - a materially different (and much harder to verify) answer. It is unclear whether this reflects (a) an intentional twist unique to the 2019 version, or (b) a memory/transcription slip by whoever wrote the reconstruction (dropping the second GenericStack<Integer> instance declaration). Given this genuine ambiguity about the actual source code, and that guessing wrongly here would actively mislead the student on a recurring exam classic, this instance was excluded rather than resolved either way.
